speaker: interviewer
text: We're Interviewing Max Rosenbloom, and we are at take 3. You were telling us about when you were segregated. Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- when we were segregated, this was 1944, sometime in August, something like that. So they were separating-- woman separate, men separate, kids, and things like that, young people. So-- and they send us from there. And there were-- there was one SS man. And I recall an incident when a mother-- a mother with a daughter-- the daughter was maybe 12, 14 years old-- and the mother didn't want to be separated from her daughter. So they were staying together, but they took the mother out someplace else. And the daughter, they want to send someplace else. And the mother was starting-- she was holding on to her daughter-- and she was starting to scream, to cry no, no, no. The SS man went over, shot them both on the spot. But the segregation-- the mother would have-- I was down there, close, maybe one meter away, standing. This was-- and then they separated us.

speaker: interviewer
text: One group, they sent I think to Buchenwald right away. And one group, which I was there, they send us to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] called a place, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] near-- near Lodz. You heard about Lodz, yeah? Near there to dig trenches because the Russians were starting coming close. And they wanted to defend themselves, so trenches, you know like military trenches. So how many were in your group that were sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the group was about 250 or something like that or more. Some of them people, which we lived through all those ordeals, they're still here. I still meet them all the time. There, we were living in-- not in barracks, they didn't have no types of barracks, but they took away those barracks-- not barracks, but the barns from the farmers. We were living there, sleeping in straw and things like-- but it wasn't long. It was about three weeks or something like that, four weeks. We were there, and then they took us away to Tschenstochau. In Tschenstochau, I was working as a carpenter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the conditions the same as it had been?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Tschenstochau had already like a camp, barracks. Because in Tschenstochau was also two camps. One was where they brought in the new people. And one was there, which they were before a long time before they were there, for about two, three years. Pelcery. They called. And the one was Czestochowianka. I was in the Czestochowianka. And there, I was working as a carpenter, helping with building more barracks. But it wasn't too bad you know. They were guarding us, you know, in the camp, but we were move freely. This was already in January 1945-- '44, no, 1945 in January.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were there in 1945. In January they made an-- in that particular Czestochowianka-- they made an appeal. Everybody out from the barracks. We're marching out from there in the barrack. We're marching. Where are we marching? We don't know. The Werkschutz were there with-- the Ukraine and Werkschutz-- with one SS man. That's about it. And we were marching. We were marching. They took us to the station-- a railway station. This was January 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, like, they saying, we were going-- all the-- the-- the Jews. There were-- something was there that everybody was so afraid that-- like mice, like anybody else, afraid for-- for nothing. 1945, when they took us out to march to this-- to the railway to send us a way, we were seeing across the street Russian soldiers fighting in the streets with Germans. We could have run away. We could have gone away, just like that, because the-- the Werkschutz, the Ukrainian Werkschutz run away already. They left us, because they were afraid for the Russians. They shouldn't capture them. They run away, and one SS man with a pistol in his hand put us into the wagons, to the kettle wagons, you know, railway. Maybe 400, 500 people-- one SS man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why? Why would people run away then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because everybody run away. You know those Werkschutz, they were guiding us. They run away. So he was the only left over. We could have gone away. We didn't have to go in there. Nobody-- He would have a chance, because if he was there, the-- the-- the end-- end of the railway could have-- everybody could have done it. But we went in like sheeps, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just because everybody was afraid-- nowhere to go. We were afraid when we're going to go out from here, and we're going to go, the first Pole which is going to see you in a striped suit, so they will know that we are from the concentration camp, they're going to kill us anyways. So what's the difference? That's what we were afraid of-- that we were going to run away and no place to run. No place to go. The Poles going to kill us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you talk amongst yourselves, or was this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we were sleeping-- we were about 20, 30, 40 people in one kettle wagon. We're sleeping together. You know, we would talk about it and everything, you know. But nobody did anything. We went in, took us to Buchenwald. In Buchenwald, we came in there about 500 people. If I can tell you, if you have left over in 500 people, maybe 10 or 20, which they survived, would be a lot. I know maybe two or three, which-- we-- we met here in Canada. But the rest everything was scattered all over. They put us to barracks. And right away from the barracks, they send us all over to other camps for work-- January, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was Buchenwald like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Buchenwald-- you know, this was the real McCoy-- nothing there, just dying-- dying and burning people. When you went out from the barrack, that's all what you've seen is-- they couldn't burn them fast enough. That's all you've seen it like pieces of wood, you know, piled liked that, and like that, and like that, that. Could hardly see the top-- dead people. They couldn't burn them fast enough. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was in Buchenwald. I was sent away to-- from there I was sent away to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Flossenburg concentration camp. So how long were you in Buchenwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Buchenwald, I was maybe three weeks-- until the end of January, not longer. So they took out from the same barrack where I was-- I still remember the barrack number 52. They came in one day, they closed the barrack. Everybody else out, out, out, out. They gathered everybody, took us to the trains, send us away to that Flossenburg. And there was the real thing, like no way out, no way of surviving. This was in the-- in a forest. They built up there, in the forest, they built up factories, ammunition factories in a hurry. The people were dying just like flies-- nothing else. And they were-- it was 1945. It was already practically the war was over. You know, another four months or three months-- not even three months. But this was the last chances, which they figured that this was going to help.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was there about until maybe from January until the middle of February. But I couldn't go on any further. I was practically-- it was-- it didn't matter to me anymore because-- You're saying physically, emotionally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Physically, emotional, everything-- I knew that this is the end. That's all. You can't go on. So they made a segregation of people which they were sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went in with the sick ones. And they send me back to Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On the wagons, we were five or six days-- something like that-- because they didn't have the right way. They was already jamming themselves together. They didn't know-- so that's why-- it should have taken maybe a day or a half a day to go. They took us five, six days-- no food, no nothing. They took about 100 to 120 people, something like that, in about three, four-- three or four kettle-- open, not closed kettle things, but open ones. No food, no nothing. When I came back to Buchenwald, I was worse off than I was there. You know, I couldn't eat anymore. I couldn't drink anymore. So they took us into a big hole. Those, the survivors, from 120, maybe 20 was left over. The rest was everything gone, because five days, six days like that undernourished , everybody was-- so I was between the ones. And they took us into a big hole, and they gave us soup with water, with things like that. I couldn't swallow or drink, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, slowly, I was taking a little bit of soup and a little bit in-- in coffee. I had a friend in Buchenwald, which he was there. And he was working with the-- with the, like [? stumm, ?] like for the barrack, like, you know, to look after the barrack and things like that. So he had a little bit more food. And he had coffee and things like that. So he gave me coffee to drink.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they gave me a little bit of food. I was there in Buchenwald again. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In the same barrack, they put me-- in barrack 52. The same what they took me out. Maybe two weeks later-- no, it wasn't even two weeks later. It was maybe a week later-- they closed up that barrack. And I was so happy in the same barrack. And they took out everybody from that barrack. Well, was they put us in trains. And they took me back. They put me away to camp called [? Dortmund. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was [? Dortmund? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Dortmund-- ?] this was also Germany. But this is near the border of Switzerland, not far from Switzerland. Some people which we came there, they were the-- in this camp, there were French men and Russian people, Russian soldiers and French people, Jews-- a lot of Jews also from Belgium, from all those countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they told us that not far is the border of Switzerland. If you want to take a chance, maybe you can run out and go over the border, you know, and go to Switzerland. There in [? Dortmund, ?] I was at work. I was injured.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were loading, because they were transporting everybody, everything they had, machine, every-- and all kind of equipments and things like that. The Germans were running from one place to another. And we were transporting baton machines-- you know, cement machine. We were transporting and putting this in-- in railway, open.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we made a platform. And the platform was not strong enough. And we started to push that heavy machine. Who know how many tons? The platform broke in the middle.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it fell on everybody, you know. I was underneath also. And about five or six people, [INAUDIBLE] it took them right out, dead. And I was very bad injured that time. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I didn't know when I got up, when I came to, I was lying in a pile of-- who knows? You couldn't count, between dead. I was there in that pile. But somehow I-- I didn't know, because I was unconscious, and things like that. Somehow I came to, and I started to cry. And the people seen, which the workers all around have seen-- look at that. Between the dead-- somebody's coming out from between the dead. So they were running over there, and they took me out. And I was already stripped from my shoes. I didn't have no shoes. I had shoes with-- with wood. You know, those Holland-- Holland shoes with wood. This was gone. I had a jacket-- a striped jacket. It was gone. I was practically naked, lying around there. They took me up, put me away in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was March, in 1945, March. And I was lying there, and I started-- I was lying, I remember, still, crying, crying, crying-- nothing else but crying. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this was March. The day was starting to get very, very dark. And the people, which they were working there, they took them away back to the barracks. And I was still lying there.

speaker: interviewer
text: One guard, whether he was a Czechoslovakian, I think, maybe, or maybe a Ukrainian, I don't recall. But the way he was speaking to me-- so he came over to me, and he says, what are you-- why you crying? Be quiet. If not, we're going to shoot you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said please, do that, because I can't survive, because this is the end. You can see-- he said-- said to me-- he gave me a piece of sugar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said to me, don't think about it. The war is soon going to be over. You're going to live. Don't think about it. So I said to him, how am I going to live? Here in the ground? In the cold ground in March? I'm going to live? I can't move.

speaker: interviewee
text: My feet were swollen like that. just as you see-- so broken. My back was broken. My ribs was broken. My [INAUDIBLE]. I was broken up to pieces, and I couldn't move.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said what's the difference to me, whether the war is going to be over? I can't survive this. He went away. He'd see what's [INAUDIBLE]. He went away with the whole group back to the barracks. And I was lying there on the ground-- March. So I got tired from crying, so I figured, what the hell? Another hour, another two hours. So it's near then. All of a sudden it got dark. From nowhere, here came, a wagon-- a small wagon with a horse and buggy. A Werkschutz, a Ukrainian, he took me, put me in that wagon. He took me back to the camp. I said, what is this? Couldn't figure out. So they took me back to the camp, and they put me in the-- in the-- in the barrack with sick people, all sick people, all dying people.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And was a doctor there. I recall this was a Polish doctor from Warsaw. I was talking to him in Polish. And he'd seen my situation. I said, do something. Take a look. So-- and I was complaining. I can't lie still. I can't do nothing, so do something. Either you do something or, you know-- one of the two. It was no use to suffer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he told me-- in Polish he told me-- if I'm not going to close my mouth-- [? as a ?] [? duty-- ?] if I'm not going to close my mouth, and if I'm going to say another word, he's going to strangle me. And you're going to be dead, he says. Don't say another word. If not, you're not going to live another hour. So I heard a thing, this in Polish, you know. And he used a rough-- like in Polish, it's very, very bad. So I figured what am I going to say? So I was went lying around there-- lying, lying. So they gave me-- once a day, they gave me a piece of bread, a little bit of soup, some water, you know, things like that. I didn't say another word.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only thing he brought me is like a bowl. He put my foot in the bowl. And he gave me a bandage, you know, just like that. And he told me lay still. That's it. This was it. In March, I was there in the hospital maybe four weeks, four or five weeks. Staying there, I couldn't go down from bed. I couldn't move. I couldn't do nothing. But they kept me there somehow. I don't know why. Because usually they didn't play around with those. You're good for nothing anymore, because if you're broken up like that, you can't work. You can't do nothing. So they cut away with you very fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you develop any hope at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was-- was-- was there hope? My hope was any day, either they're going to kill me, or they'll take me out to shot-- to shoot, like I had experienced with my brother. Or they're going-- You can't. You can't. What does the dead hope for? The only hope was they had to everyday, if they gave you a piece of bread, you were lucky. That's it. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was lying there maybe three, four weeks, something like that. In April, it got very bad already. The Germans were-- they were squeezed from all over-- from the Russian side, from the American side, from all over the side. So they started to evacuate all the camps. So from this camp, they took me out to Dachau. Also they-- I couldn't walk. So they brought a wagon, and they took some people, which they were supposed to be evacuated too. They wheeled me over to the railway, and they put in the railway. So from there-- from that-- from that transport also, maybe the whole-- the whole camp, they liquidated it.

speaker: interviewee
text: They sent them all over. So I was lucky enough to be Buchenwald-- not in Buchenwald, in Dachau-- not Dachau-- was called Allach. This is like the foreground before you go into Dachau. So I came there. The-- the time which were transported was about four days, four or five days. Also nothing to eat, nothing, nothing. And they took us out to Dachau. And they took me out on a stretcher. They took me out to the-- to the gas chamber, closer to the gas chamber, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like, I've seen the [? homes ?] there and everything, the burning. Well, who cares? So they took me out there. And before they put you into the gas chamber, and things like that, so they shaved you. I had hair, you know, not like that. So they shaved me. And they had there barbers-- Jewish barbers, two, three, as many as fast as go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came to me a guy, which-- a [? landsman ?] of mine, which he knew me from-- from the-- from the city of Staszow, because I was-- as a youngster, I remember I was going to his barber shop in Staszow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name? [? Plotnich ?] or something like that, you know, was his name. So he takes a look at me. What are you doing here? They're going to gas you. So? I don't care. What can I do? He says, take a look at me. I said, take a look. I can't walk, I can't run, I can't do nothing. So I'm dead. So what's the difference? So he called two guys, and they put me in the same stretcher, and they took me out to a back door from that gas chamber. They took me out in the back door, and they put me in the barracks. This was the end of April. And they put me into a barrack.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was in that barrack. And then came the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] few days later, I think, maybe two weeks later, something like that, the Americans came in-- May 8th. This was my luck. Probably supposed to be like that, and I'm supposed to live. This is it. And then the American came in. So I was-- they came in with the registrations-- where you were born, how old you are, and everything. So I told them I was born in Poland, how old I am, things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So a few days later, they took me out, and they sent me to a camp, which was only Poles there. And they gathered them, and they sent-- they were sending them away to Poland, back to Poland. You were born Poland? You are Pole? Sending them back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Called-- that camp was called [GERMAN] And from there, I heard they're going to send me away. I sneak out at night. I sneak out. I couldn't walk. Hardly I could walk. I was with a cane. I was practically crawling in my feet and hand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you sneak out? Why didn't you want to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I didn't want to go to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to go to Poland. Afterwards, I went to Poland anyways. But this time, I didn't want that they should send me with transports with everything. So I sneak out, and I went to [GERMAN] Germany. I went in there to hospital. And they examined me and things like that. They said OK, you can-- but we haven't got a bed for you. We can't keep you here, but we can give you treatment as much as we can do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So to stay there and wait for treatments, they gave me some food. You know, this is that right after the war. And they gave me some pills to take for the pain. And I heard that there is a camp for living. And I ask directions. So they told me take this and this route and this. And you go to the station, and you ask there. And they going to take you to Feldafing. So I did so. And I came to Feldafing.

speaker: interviewee
text: I met a [? landsman ?] of mine at the station, which you know, not a [? landsman ?] just, but a guy which we were going to school together. He's still alive. But he's-- from the war-- he got hit in his head. And he was in [INAUDIBLE] for quite a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Motl. Motl-- you know, it's. I got him in the calendar. If you want to know exactly, I'll tell you his name. So he talked. He says, what are you doing here? So I said, you can see what I'm doing here. They told me to come here, that here is a camp, and I can get some treatment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took me to the barracks. Laid down there, gave me some food, things like that. In the morning, to the barracks, there were always coming nurses with doctors to see who is sick and who needs help. So they came to me, and they examined me. And right away phoned an ambulance. They took me to the hospital in Feldafing. I was in the hospital for maybe till-- this was May, June-- about until the end of August. August, September I was there in the hospital. And they wanted to do all kinds of operations on me.

speaker: interviewee
text: With the American doctors, the German doctors came in the examining. And they made x-rays, and they wanted to break my leg. They wanted to break my back. They put in all kinds of treatments. Cut out my ribs. You know, they told me what they want to do-- everything. And they gave me a paper to sign. He said, we're going to schedule because there was a lot of-- a lot of Jewish young kids, you know. Like, I was there in hospital. In the one room, I was with a guy with two brothers-- castrated. They were in-- in-- in-- the concentration camp. I think they were also in Dachau. They were castrated. And they were suffering very badly, you know. I was with them in the same room in the hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to have to stop here and continue on with the next tape to finish the story.
